items.myth: Plotly replaces Matplotlib and Seaborn entirely in an ML stack.
items.reality: Plotly adds an interactive layer for exploration and HTML dashboards; Matplotlib and Seaborn remain the canonical path for static publication figures and many statistical defaults on the same data.
items.example: A paper figure uses fig.savefig from Matplotlib; the same project uses px.scatter + write_html for internal misclassification review.

items.myth: Plotly Express and Graph Objects are unrelated APIs.
items.reality: Express functions return Graph Objects Figure instances—you can call fig.update_layout, fig.add_trace, and fig.write_html on Express output.
items.example: fig = px.line(df, x='epoch', y='loss', color='run'); fig.update_layout(hovermode='x unified') mixes both layers.

items.myth: Hover tooltips appear automatically with every useful ML field.
items.reality: Default hovers show mapped x/y/color fields only; extra metadata requires hover_data (Express) or customdata + hovertemplate (GO).
items.example: sample_id is invisible on hover until you pass hover_data=['sample_id'] in px.scatter.

items.myth: Plotly owns embedding interpretation and chart-type selection.
items.reality: Plotly supplies interactive drawing primitives for coordinate plots; embedding visualization owns layout interpretation, and choosing chart types owns which visualization fits the question.
items.example: px.scatter_3d draws rotatable coordinates; whether 3D is misleading vs 2D belongs in embedding visualization, not here.
